%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
## Overview

> **Objective:** Understand how complex numbers are represented, manipulated algebraically, and why this matters for solving real-world engineering problems.

Complex numbers are not just abstract curiosities — they are a powerful shortcut. As your textbook puts it, most real-world problems start and end with real numbers, but the path through complex numbers is far shorter. Section B.1-2 focuses on the **algebra** of complex numbers: how to represent them graphically on the complex plane, how to switch between rectangular form \(z = a + jb\) and polar form \(z = re^{j\theta}\), and how to perform operations like addition, multiplication, conjugation, and finding magnitude. After this section, you will be able to convert between forms fluently and recognize which form is most useful for a given problem.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Fig. B.2](/figures/page-005-fig__b_2-1.png)
*Fig. B.2 shows the complex plane with \(z = a + jb\) as a point at coordinates \((a, b)\), its magnitude \(r\) and angle \(\theta\), and its conjugate \(z^* = a - jb\) reflected across the real axis — this single diagram anchors every algebraic operation in this section.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="comparison_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 Rectangular form \(z = a + jb\) and polar form \(z = re^{j\theta}\) are two descriptions of the same point — this diagram shows how every parameter maps between them, and flags the conjugate sign-change as the most common exam trap.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777214446036-5665.png)

## Core Idea

A complex number has **two equivalent representations**, and fluency in this section means knowing when and how to switch between them.

**Rectangular form:** \(z = a + jb\), where \(a = \text{Re}(z)\) is the real part and \(b = \text{Im}(z)\) is the imaginary part. Note carefully: \(\text{Im}(z) = b\), not \(jb\) — the imaginary part is a real number.

**Polar / exponential form:** \(z = re^{j\theta}\), where \(r = |z| = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}\) is the magnitude and \(\theta = \arctan(b/a)\) is the angle.

The **complex conjugate** \(z^* = a - jb = re^{-j\theta}\) simply flips the sign of the imaginary part (or the angle). Its most important property is:

$$zz^* = |z|^2 = r^2$$

**Minimal example:** For \(z = 3 + j4\), we get \(r = \sqrt{9 + 16} = 5\), \(\theta = \arctan(4/3) \approx 53.1°\), and \(z^* = 3 - j4\).

### COMMON EXAM TRAP

Students often write \(\text{Im}(z) = jb\) instead of \(\text{Im}(z) = b\). The imaginary part is always a **real number** — the \(j\) is not included.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- A complex number \(z = a + jb\) is a point on the 2D complex plane with real part \(a\) and imaginary part \(b\).
- Polar form \(z = re^{j\theta}\) encodes the same point using magnitude \(r\) and angle \(\theta\) — both forms are equivalent.
- The conjugate \(z^* = a - jb\) flips the imaginary sign; the key identity \(zz^* = |z|^2\) appears constantly in exams.

*In the next section we will apply these representations to Euler's formula and explore how exponential form makes multiplication and division of complex numbers elegantly simple.*
